steps.title: Identify the rhetorical goal
steps.content: The student wants to introduce both Samuel Selvon and his novel The Lonely Londoners to a new audience. The effective choice must name Selvon, identify him, name the novel, and give the audience enough context to know what it is about.

steps.title: Test the candidates against the goal
steps.content: The right choice will pack in author identity (Trinidadian), novel title, publication year, and subject (post-WWII Caribbean migration to Britain) in a single introductory sentence.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'In 1956, Trinidadian author Samuel Selvon published one of his most celebrated novels...': names Selvon, identifies him as Trinidadian, names the novel, gives the year, and summarizes its subject. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Samuel Selvon wrote the novel Moses Ascending after he wrote The Lonely Londoners': misreads the goal — it focuses on a later novel and tells a new audience nothing about who Selvon is or what The Lonely Londoners is about. ✗
  • Choice C — 'a celebrated novel that was published in 1956, depicts post-World War II Caribbean migration from the perspective of a Trinidadian author': never names Samuel Selvon, so it fails to introduce the author. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Some of the characters who appear in...Moses Ascending also appear in The Lonely Londoners': misreads the goal — it is an intertextual detail, not an introduction, and gives no information about Selvon or what the novel is about. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
